stories.headline: Job seekers warned about AI résumé mistakes
stories.summary: Job-search experts offer guidance on using artificial intelligence tools for résumés, cover letters, and networking. The advice highlights common pitfalls to avoid while leveraging AI technology as an assistant rather than a replacement in the job application process.
stories.original_title: 6 mistakes job seekers should avoid when using AI for résumés, cover letters, and networking
stories.context: Artificial Intelligence has become widely available to regular people through tools that can write text when given simple instructions. Many job seekers recently started using these programs to automatically write their résumés and cover letters, hoping to save time and sound more professional. However, these AI tools sometimes make mistakes, write things that aren't true, or create generic documents that all look exactly the same to employers. Hiring managers have become wise to this trend and can easily spot when someone's application materials were written entirely by a computer rather than the actual person, which can hurt rather than help their chances of getting hired.
stories.why_it_matters: Artificial intelligence is changing how people write résumés and find jobs. Using these tools wrongly can make job seekers look lazy or dishonest, hurting their chances of getting hired.
stories.other_side: Some people worry that too many people will use AI to cheat on job applications, making it harder for employers to find honest candidates.

stories.headline: Oracle layoffs victim's positive outlook goes viral
stories.summary: A former Oracle employee's composed and strategic response to being laid off has gained widespread attention online. Thousands were let go by the global tech giant, but this individual's focus on severance packages and future planning has resonated with others in the tech industry.
stories.original_title: Techie's response to Oracle job loss goes viral: 'I'll go all in…'
stories.context: The technology industry has experienced massive fluctuations over the past few years, seeing an explosive hiring boom followed by dramatic job cuts as companies try to reduce costs and become more efficient. Oracle, one of the world's oldest and largest software companies that helps businesses manage their data and operations, recently joined this trend by laying off thousands of workers. Losing a job unexpectedly is incredibly stressful and emotional, but this particular employee chose to view it practically, focusing on negotiating their exit package and planning their next steps rather than panicking. Their calm and strategic response struck a chord with many other tech workers who have faced similar situations or worry about their own job security in this volatile industry.
stories.why_it_matters: Mass layoffs in the tech industry are happening more often, affecting thousands of workers. This story shows people how to handle job loss with grace and preparation instead of panic.
stories.other_side: Critics say this positive reaction doesn't change the fact that big companies are cutting jobs without caring about their workers' lives.

stories.headline: SaaS on the Beach conference returns with founder-only format
stories.summary: The SaaS on the Beach tech conference will return to Barcelona in May with a unique founder-only format. The event distinguishes itself from crowded tech conferences by focusing on fewer attendees and eliminating sales presentations to create more meaningful networking opportunities.
stories.original_title: SaaS on the Beach returns to Barcelona with a founder-only format
stories.context: Technology conferences have traditionally been massive, crowded events where hundreds or thousands of people gather to listen to presentations, browse exhibits, and try to make business connections. Most of these large events are filled with sales pitches from companies trying to sell their products to attendees, which can make it difficult to have meaningful conversations or form genuine relationships. SaaS stands for "Software as a Service," which means companies that sell software that customers access online rather than installing it on their own computers. This specific conference decided to do the opposite of the norm by limiting attendance only to company founders and removing all sales presentations, creating a more intimate environment where leaders can share experiences and partnerships without the noise and pressure of traditional tech events.
stories.why_it_matters: Most tech conferences are overcrowded sales pitches that waste people's time. This smaller, founder-only event shows how meaningful business connections happen when people actually talk instead of listening to commercials.
stories.other_side: Some argue that limiting events to founders only excludes other important people in companies who could benefit from networking and learning.

stories.headline: UK startup secures funding for EV battery refinery
stories.summary: Altilium, a UK clean technology company, has obtained £18.5 million in government grant funding to build Britain's first commercial refinery for electric vehicle battery recycling. The project aims to recover critical minerals from end-of-life EV batteries as the automotive industry transitions to electric power.
stories.original_title: UK startup Altilium bags £18.5m to build Britain's first commercial EV battery refinery
stories.context: The world's car manufacturers are rapidly switching from vehicles powered by gasoline to those powered by electricity to help fight climate change and reduce pollution. Electric vehicles run on large batteries containing valuable and expensive minerals like lithium, cobalt, and nickel that need to be mined from the earth. When these batteries reach the end of their life, many people worry about creating dangerous waste or losing access to these critical materials. A refinery is a facility that processes raw materials to extract and purify valuable components, so building one specifically for old batteries means collecting used batteries, breaking them down safely, and recovering those precious minerals to be used again in new batteries instead of throwing everything away or mining more from the ground.
stories.why_it_matters: Electric cars need batteries made from rare materials that run out quickly. Building plants to reuse old batteries helps protect the environment and makes electric cars more sustainable for everyone.
stories.other_side: Some opponents worry that government money is being spent on unproven technology that might not work as well as planned.

stories.headline: Onyx Boox releases software update for e-reader devices
stories.summary: Onyx Boox has released version 4.2 firmware update for its Note Air 5C and Go 10.3 Gen 2 Lumi e-reader devices. The software update brings various new features and enhancements to improve the user experience on these digital reading devices.
stories.original_title: New Software update for Note Air5 C and Go 10.3 (Gen II) Lumi
stories.context: E-readers are electronic tablets designed specifically for reading digital books, often using special screens that look like paper and don't hurt your eyes like regular computer or phone screens. Onyx Boox is a company that makes these reading devices, and like with any computer or smartphone, the software inside them needs periodic updates to fix problems and add new abilities. Firmware is the permanent software programmed into the device that controls how it works, similar to the operating system on a computer. When companies release firmware updates, they typically include improvements like better battery life, smoother performance, new features that customers have been asking for, or fixes for bugs that were causing problems for users.
stories.why_it_matters: Electronic reading devices need regular software updates to work better and fix problems. This update helps people who use these devices have a smoother and more enjoyable reading experience.
stories.other_side: Critics argue that companies should release devices that work perfectly from the start instead of requiring constant updates to fix issues.

stories.headline: OpenAI identifies third-party tool security vulnerability
stories.summary: OpenAI, the maker of ChatGPT, has discovered and disclosed a security issue involving a third-party tool connected to its systems. The company stated that its investigation found no evidence of user data access, system compromise, or intellectual property theft resulting from the security incident.
stories.original_title: OpenAI identifies security issue involving third-party tool
stories.context: OpenAI is the company that created ChatGPT, which is a program that can have conversations, answer questions, and help with writing tasks using artificial intelligence. Many modern software programs don't try to do everything themselves but instead connect to other smaller tools and services to accomplish specific tasks, kind of like how a smartphone might connect to your email account or banking app. A third-party tool means software created by a different company that plugs into or works alongside the main program. A security vulnerability is like a digital lock that doesn't work properly—it's a weakness that could let someone break in who shouldn't have access. OpenAI discovered that one of these connected tools had such a weakness, investigated it carefully, and told everyone about it so the problem could be fixed before any harm could be done.
stories.why_it_matters: AI companies like OpenAI handle personal information from millions of users. Finding and fixing security problems before data gets stolen helps keep everyone's private information safe from hackers.
stories.other_side: Some people worry that AI companies can't be trusted to find all security problems themselves and need outside experts to check their work.

stories.headline: OpenAI CEO's California home targeted in security incident
stories.summary: A man has been arrested following an incident at the California residence of OpenAI's CEO Sam Altman. The company behind ChatGPT confirmed that no one was injured during the event but did not disclose whether the CEO was home at the time or provide details on the suspect's motives.
stories.original_title: OpenAI CEO's California home attacked, man arrested
stories.context: Sam Altman became one of the most famous and influential people in the technology world as the leader of OpenAI, the company behind ChatGPT, which revolutionized how people think about artificial intelligence. When someone becomes extremely wealthy or powerful in the public eye, they sometimes attract unwanted attention from people who might mentally fixate on them. Security incidents can range from fans trying to meet them to genuine threats from people with harmful intentions. When these events happen at private homes, it raises serious concerns about safety and privacy not just for famous individuals but for their families and neighbors as well, leading companies to enhance security measures and reconsider how much information they make public about where their executives live and work.
stories.why_it_matters: Leaders of powerful AI companies are becoming targets for people who are angry or confused about artificial intelligence. This shows how the debate over AI is turning into real-world danger for the people building these technologies.
stories.other_side: Privacy advocates say making these incidents public news only draws more unwanted attention to executives and their families.

stories.headline: Estonia opposes EU children's social media restrictions
stories.summary: Estonia has emerged as one of only two European Union member states to reject a pan-European declaration restricting children's access to social media platforms. The country's ministers argue that age-based bans are unenforceable and advocate for different approaches to online safety.
stories.original_title: Estonia is the rare EU country opposing bans on children's social media use
stories.context: The European Union is a partnership of 27 European countries that work together on many issues, including making laws that affect all member states to keep people safe across borders. Parents and governments everywhere have grown increasingly worried about children spending too much time on social media platforms like TikTok and Instagram, where they might see harmful content, get bullied, or become addicted. Some countries have tried to solve this problem by creating laws that ban children under certain ages from using these platforms at all, but enforcing these rules is incredibly difficult since kids can easily lie about their age or find ways around the restrictions. Estonia, a small but technologically advanced European nation, believes that outright bans don't work and waste time trying to police them, arguing instead that society should focus on better education and safety features built into the platforms themselves rather than trying to block access entirely.
stories.why_it_matters: European governments are trying to decide if kids should be banned from social media websites. Estonia's stand against these bans shows that blocking access doesn't work and might actually be impossible to enforce.
stories.other_side: Supporters of the ban say protecting children from harmful content online is more important than worrying about whether the rules can be perfectly enforced.

stories.headline: 5paisa Capital's rights issue oversubscribed amid tech sector interest
stories.summary: 5paisa Capital's ₹469 crore rights issue garnered bids for 1.93 crore shares against 1.56 crore shares offered, resulting in 1.24 times oversubscription. The financial technology company's successful offering reflects continued investor appetite in the fintech sector.
stories.original_title: 5paisa Capital's ₹469 crore rights issue oversubscribed 1.24 times
stories.context: A rights issue is when a company raises money by selling new shares of stock only to its existing shareholders, giving them the right to buy more shares before anyone else can. Fintech combines the words "financial" and "technology," referring to companies that use technology to provide financial services like investing, banking, or managing money in new and different ways than traditional banks. When a company offers shares for sale, being oversubscribed means investors wanted to buy more shares than the company was actually selling, which is a good sign that people have confidence in the company's future. 5paisa Capital is an Indian company that lets people invest in stocks online through apps and websites, and investors are clearly excited about this type of business, willing to put their money into the company even as technology stocks face uncertainty in other parts of the world.
stories.why_it_matters: Investors are still putting money into companies that build financial technology apps. This shows that people believe the future of banking and investing will happen through smartphones and computers.
stories.other_side: Some financial experts worry that too much money flowing into fintech companies could create another bubble that might burst and hurt regular investors.

stories.headline: AI pioneer discusses multi-agent automation for executive tasks
stories.summary: Illia Polosukhin, co-author of the influential transformer AI research paper, describes how he deploys approximately a dozen AI agents to handle various tasks. The AI expert argues that institutions must better prepare for the growing capabilities of autonomous AI agents in business and organizational contexts.
stories.original_title: 'A billionaire's chief of staff': How this AI pioneer puts a dozen agents to work
stories.context: The transformer is a type of computer system design that became the foundation for modern AI programs like ChatGPT and other tools that can understand and generate human language remarkably well. Illia Polosukhin helped write the original research paper that introduced this design, making him one of the pioneers whose work made today's AI boom possible. An AI agent is a computer program that can go off and complete tasks on its own rather than just answering questions when you type them in—like having a digital assistant that can send emails, schedule meetings, or research topics without your direct involvement for every step. The idea of using multiple AI agents together means having several different specialized programs simultaneously working on different parts of a complex job, similar to how a business might have different employees handling different aspects of a project, which could dramatically change how companies operate and how many human workers they need.
stories.why_it_matters: AI programs are getting smart enough to work as teams that do office jobs for us. This will change how businesses operate and could automate work that people do today, potentially changing entire industries.
stories.other_side: Some experts fear that companies will replace too many human workers with AI agents, causing job losses and economic problems for regular people.
